edges.evidence_phrase: Scanning electron microscopy was used to qualitatively assess surface roughness (SEM, Zeiss Auriga, Carl Zeiss, Germany), and confocal microscopy was used for quantitative assessment (LSM 980 Laser Scanning Microscope, Germany) with a 20x objective and a total measurement area of 790 um x 790 um (n=3). A moving average Gaussian filter with a 30 um cut-off wavelength was used to calculate the arithmetic mean height of the scale-limited surface (Sa). Three samples were used for each surface type to calculate the mean surface roughness at six different areas. A Ram-Hart goniometer was used to measure the sessile drop contact angle to determine surface hydrophilicity (Model 100-25a, Rame-Hart Instrument Co., Succasunna, NJ). 5 uL drops of deionized water were used to measure surface hydrophilicity at six locations per sample (n=3). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a PhI5000 VersaProbe spectrometer (ULVAC-PHI, Inc.) to determine the oxide layer composition. A focused scanning monochromatic Al-Ka source (1,486.6 eV) with a spot size of 200 um was used to acquire spectra, utilizing a base pressure of 1x10 -7  Pa. FAT analyzer mode was used to run the instrument. Detail spectra of 187.85 eV and 46.95 eV were used for the pass energy for survey scans. CasaXPS was utilized for data analysis. Shirley background subtraction was used to integrate the signals. Measurements were taken on each sample in three separate areas.
edges.source: Biomaterial Surface Engineering:Surface roughness
edges.target: Biomaterial Surface Engineering:Surface hydrophilicity

edges.evidence_phrase: Biomaterial surface modifications like roughness and wettability can dictate the recruitment and behavior of immune cells at the implant-tissue interface. Previously, we and others have shown that innate immune cells, including neutrophils and macrophages, activate and polarize in response to differences in Ti physicochemical cues in vitro and in vivo [22,25,32,34,35]. Additionally, we have shown that T cell polarization in vitro and in vivo also responds to changes in biomaterial surface roughness and wettability through interactions with macrophages [21,25,27]. This study demonstrates that environmental factors such as obesity significantly impact the inflammatory response and polarization of immune cells within the peri-implant microenvironment. Our data shows that neutrophils, macrophages, T cells, and MSC recruitment are significantly affected by obesity. Here, we utilized rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants to demonstrate that obesity attenuates the anti-inflammatory and pro-osseointegrative response associated with increasing surface wettability on modified Ti implants.
edges.source: Biomaterial Surface Engineering:Surface roughness
edges.target: Biomaterial Surface Engineering:Titanium implants

edges.evidence_phrase: Biomaterial surface modifications like roughness and wettability can dictate the recruitment and behavior of immune cells at the implant-tissue interface. Previously, we and others have shown that innate immune cells, including neutrophils and macrophages, activate and polarize in response to differences in Ti physicochemical cues in vitro and in vivo [22,25,32,34,35]. Additionally, we have shown that T cell polarization in vitro and in vivo also responds to changes in biomaterial surface roughness and wettability through interactions with macrophages [21,25,27]. This study demonstrates that environmental factors such as obesity significantly impact the inflammatory response and polarization of immune cells within the peri-implant microenvironment. Our data shows that neutrophils, macrophages, T cells, and MSC recruitment are significantly affected by obesity. Here, we utilized rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants to demonstrate that obesity attenuates the anti-inflammatory and pro-osseointegrative response associated with increasing surface wettability on modified Ti implants.
edges.source: Biomaterial Surface Engineering:Wettability
edges.target: Biomaterial Surface Engineering:Titanium implants

A Ram-Hart goniometer was used to measure the sessile drop contact angle to determine surface hydrophilicity (Model 100-25a, Rame-Hart Instrument Co., Succasunna, NJ). 5 uL drops of deionized water were used to measure surface hydrophilicity at six locations per sample (n=3). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a PhI5000 VersaProbe spectrometer (ULVAC-PHI, Inc.) to determine the oxide layer composition. A focused scanning monochromatic Al-Ka source (1,486.6 eV) with a spot size of 200 um was used to acquire spectra, utilizing a base pressure of 1x10 -7  Pa. FAT analyzer mode was used to run the instrument. Detail spectra of 187.85 eV and 46.95 eV were used for the pass energy for survey scans. CasaXPS was utilized for data analysis. Shirley background subtraction was used to integrate the signals. Measurements were taken on each sample in three separate areas.

edges.evidence_phrase: To understand the effect of obesity on the polarization of macrophages in vitro, we cultured bone marrow-derived macrophages from obese or lean mice on rough and roughhydrophilic Ti disks. The proportion of lean-derived pro-inflammatory macrophages was lower in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti surfaces than rough Ti (Figure 10). In contrast, anti-inflammatory macrophages were higher in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti. Obesederived pro-inflammatory macrophages were significantly elevated in response to both surfaces, with the highest populations occurring in response to rough Ti. Obese-derived anti-inflammatory macrophages were lower on all substrates when compared to lean-derived macrophages.
edges.source: Macrophage Polarization and Immune Response:Pro-inflammatory macrophages
edges.target: Macrophage Polarization and Immune Response:Anti-inflammatory macrophages

edges.evidence_phrase: Obesity is a metabolic disease resulting from excess body fat accumulation associated with chronic systemic inflammation. Obesity has been shown to impact the function and activity of neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells, contributing to higher circulating levels of proinflammatory cytokines. Biomaterial surface properties such as roughness and hydrophilicity can influence the behavior of immune cells in the peri-implant microenvironment. This study aimed to determine how obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) affects the inflammatory response to modified titanium (Ti) implants and subsequent bone formation. Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.

edges.target: Macrophage Polarization and Immune Response:Pro-inflammatory cytokines

edges.evidence_phrase: To understand the effect of obesity on the polarization of macrophages in vitro, we cultured bone marrow-derived macrophages from obese or lean mice on rough and roughhydrophilic Ti disks. The proportion of lean-derived pro-inflammatory macrophages was lower in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti surfaces than rough Ti (Figure 10). In contrast, anti-inflammatory macrophages were higher in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti. Obesederived pro-inflammatory macrophages were significantly elevated in response to both surfaces, with the highest populations occurring in response to rough Ti. Obese-derived anti-inflammatory macrophages were lower on all substrates when compared to lean-derived macrophages.

edges.evidence_phrase: Neutrophils and macrophages are myeloid-derived cells that clear wound debris via phagocytosis and initiate the immune response [24,25]. Neutrophils release inflammatory mediators like myeloperoxidase (MPO), neutrophil elastase, and neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs). Macrophages differentiate into pro- or anti-inflammatory macrophages depending on the biochemical and physical cues in the inflammatory microenvironment [25,26]. Our group has demonstrated that macrophage polarization is required to facilitate the amplification and resolution of the inflammatory response to implanted metallic biomaterials and orchestrate the recruitment of T cells and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) using titanium (Ti) as a model [22,25]. Ablating macrophages during the inflammatory response to Ti implants significantly attenuates T cell and MSC recruitment, compromising tissue regeneration [22].
edges.source: Macrophage Polarization and Immune Response:Neutrophils
edges.target: Macrophage Polarization and Immune Response:T cells

edges.evidence_phrase: Biomaterial surface modifications like roughness and wettability can dictate the recruitment and behavior of immune cells at the implant-tissue interface. Previously, we and others have shown that innate immune cells, including neutrophils and macrophages, activate and polarize in response to differences in Ti physicochemical cues in vitro and in vivo [22,25,32,34,35]. Additionally, we have shown that T cell polarization in vitro and in vivo also responds to changes in biomaterial surface roughness and wettability through interactions with macrophages [21,25,27]. This study demonstrates that environmental factors such as obesity significantly impact the inflammatory response and polarization of immune cells within the peri-implant microenvironment. Our data shows that neutrophils, macrophages, T cells, and MSC recruitment are significantly affected by obesity. Here, we utilized rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants to demonstrate that obesity attenuates the anti-inflammatory and pro-osseointegrative response associated with increasing surface wettability on modified Ti implants.
edges.source: Macrophage Polarization and Immune Response:Immune cell recruitment
edges.target: Biomaterial Surface Engineering:Wettability

edges.evidence_phrase: Biomaterial surface modifications like roughness and wettability can dictate the recruitment and behavior of immune cells at the implant-tissue interface. Previously, we and others have shown that innate immune cells, including neutrophils and macrophages, activate and polarize in response to differences in Ti physicochemical cues in vitro and in vivo [22,25,32,34,35]. Additionally, we have shown that T cell polarization in vitro and in vivo also responds to changes in biomaterial surface roughness and wettability through interactions with macrophages [21,25,27]. This study demonstrates that environmental factors such as obesity significantly impact the inflammatory response and polarization of immune cells within the peri-implant microenvironment. Our data shows that neutrophils, macrophages, T cells, and MSC recruitment are significantly affected by obesity. Here, we utilized rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants to demonstrate that obesity attenuates the anti-inflammatory and pro-osseointegrative response associated with increasing surface wettability on modified Ti implants.
edges.source: Macrophage Polarization and Immune Response:Immune cell recruitment
edges.target: Osseointegration and Tissue Regeneration:Pro-osseointegrative response

edges.evidence_phrase: Obesity is a metabolic disease resulting from excess body fat accumulation associated with chronic systemic inflammation. Obesity has been shown to impact the function and activity of neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells, contributing to higher circulating levels of proinflammatory cytokines. Biomaterial surface properties such as roughness and hydrophilicity can influence the behavior of immune cells in the peri-implant microenvironment. This study aimed to determine how obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) affects the inflammatory response to modified titanium (Ti) implants and subsequent bone formation. Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.
edges.source: Macrophage Polarization and Immune Response:Pro-inflammatory cytokines
edges.target: Macrophage Polarization and Immune Response:Pro-inflammatory macrophages

Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.

Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.

edges.evidence_phrase: Biomaterial surface modifications like roughness and wettability can dictate the recruitment and behavior of immune cells at the implant-tissue interface. Previously, we and others have shown that innate immune cells, including neutrophils and macrophages, activate and polarize in response to differences in Ti physicochemical cues in vitro and in vivo [22,25,32,34,35]. Additionally, we have shown that T cell polarization in vitro and in vivo also responds to changes in biomaterial surface roughness and wettability through interactions with macrophages [21,25,27]. This study demonstrates that environmental factors such as obesity significantly impact the inflammatory response and polarization of immune cells within the peri-implant microenvironment. Our data shows that neutrophils, macrophages, T cells, and MSC recruitment are significantly affected by obesity. Here, we utilized rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants to demonstrate that obesity attenuates the anti-inflammatory and pro-osseointegrative response associated with increasing surface wettability on modified Ti implants.
edges.source: Osseointegration and Tissue Regeneration:Pro-osseointegrative response
edges.target: Biomaterial Surface Engineering:Wettability

edges.evidence_phrase: Scanning electron microscopy was used to qualitatively assess surface roughness (SEM, Zeiss Auriga, Carl Zeiss, Germany), and confocal microscopy was used for quantitative assessment (LSM 980 Laser Scanning Microscope, Germany) with a 20x objective and a total measurement area of 790 um x 790 um (n=3). A moving average Gaussian filter with a 30 um cut-off wavelength was used to calculate the arithmetic mean height of the scale-limited surface (Sa). Three samples were used for each surface type to calculate the mean surface roughness at six different areas. A Ram-Hart goniometer was used to measure the sessile drop contact angle to determine surface hydrophilicity (Model 100-25a, Rame-Hart Instrument Co., Succasunna, NJ). 5 uL drops of deionized water were used to measure surface hydrophilicity at six locations per sample (n=3). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a PhI5000 VersaProbe spectrometer (ULVAC-PHI, Inc.) to determine the oxide layer composition. A focused scanning monochromatic Al-Ka source (1,486.6 eV) with a spot size of 200 um was used to acquire spectra, utilizing a base pressure of 1x10 -7  Pa. FAT analyzer mode was used to run the instrument. Detail spectra of 187.85 eV and 46.95 eV were used for the pass energy for survey scans. CasaXPS was utilized for data analysis. Shirley background subtraction was used to integrate the signals. Measurements were taken on each sample in three separate areas.
edges.source: Biomaterial Surface Engineering:Oxide layer composition
edges.target: Biomaterial Surface Engineering:Surface hydrophilicity

edges.evidence_phrase: To understand the effect of obesity on the polarization of macrophages in vitro, we cultured bone marrow-derived macrophages from obese or lean mice on rough and roughhydrophilic Ti disks. The proportion of lean-derived pro-inflammatory macrophages was lower in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti surfaces than rough Ti (Figure 10). In contrast, anti-inflammatory macrophages were higher in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti. Obesederived pro-inflammatory macrophages were significantly elevated in response to both surfaces, with the highest populations occurring in response to rough Ti. Obese-derived anti-inflammatory macrophages were lower on all substrates when compared to lean-derived macrophages.
edges.source: Biomaterial Surface Engineering:Rough-hydrophilic titanium implants
edges.target: Macrophage Polarization and Immune Response:Pro-inflammatory macrophages

Our group has demonstrated that macrophage polarization is required to facilitate the amplification and resolution of the inflammatory response to implanted metallic biomaterials and orchestrate the recruitment of T cells and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) using titanium (Ti) as a model [22,25]. Ablating macrophages during the inflammatory response to Ti implants significantly attenuates T cell and MSC recruitment, compromising tissue regeneration [22].
edges.source: Macrophage Polarization and Immune Response:Macrophage polarization
edges.target: Macrophage Polarization and Immune Response:T cells

edges.evidence_phrase: Neutrophils and macrophages are myeloid-derived cells that clear wound debris via phagocytosis and initiate the immune response [24,25]. Neutrophils release inflammatory mediators like myeloperoxidase (MPO), neutrophil elastase, and neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs). Macrophages differentiate into pro- or anti-inflammatory macrophages depending on the biochemical and physical cues in the inflammatory microenvironment [25,26]. Our group has demonstrated that macrophage polarization is required to facilitate the amplification and resolution of the inflammatory response to implanted metallic biomaterials and orchestrate the recruitment of T cells and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) using titanium (Ti) as a model [22,25]. Ablating macrophages during the inflammatory response to Ti implants significantly attenuates T cell and MSC recruitment, compromising tissue regeneration [22].
edges.source: Macrophage Polarization and Immune Response:Macrophage polarization
edges.target: Osseointegration and Tissue Regeneration:Tissue regeneration

edges.evidence_phrase: Obesity is a metabolic disease resulting from excess body fat accumulation associated with chronic systemic inflammation. Obesity has been shown to impact the function and activity of neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells, contributing to higher circulating levels of proinflammatory cytokines. Biomaterial surface properties such as roughness and hydrophilicity can influence the behavior of immune cells in the peri-implant microenvironment. This study aimed to determine how obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) affects the inflammatory response to modified titanium (Ti) implants and subsequent bone formation. Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.
edges.source: Macrophage Polarization and Immune Response:Circulating adipokines
edges.target: Macrophage Polarization and Immune Response:Pro-inflammatory cytokines

edges.evidence_phrase: To understand the effect of obesity on the polarization of macrophages in vitro, we cultured bone marrow-derived macrophages from obese or lean mice on rough and roughhydrophilic Ti disks. The proportion of lean-derived pro-inflammatory macrophages was lower in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti surfaces than rough Ti (Figure 10). In contrast, anti-inflammatory macrophages were higher in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti. Obesederived pro-inflammatory macrophages were significantly elevated in response to both surfaces, with the highest populations occurring in response to rough Ti. Obese-derived anti-inflammatory macrophages were lower on all substrates when compared to lean-derived macrophages.
edges.source: Biomaterial Surface Properties and Macrophage Polarization:rough Ti surface
edges.target: Biomaterial Surface Properties and Macrophage Polarization:rough-hydrophilic Ti surface

edges.evidence_phrase: Obesity is a metabolic disease resulting from excess body fat accumulation associated with chronic systemic inflammation. Obesity has been shown to impact the function and activity of neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells, contributing to higher circulating levels of proinflammatory cytokines. Biomaterial surface properties such as roughness and hydrophilicity can influence the behavior of immune cells in the peri-implant microenvironment. This study aimed to determine how obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) affects the inflammatory response to modified titanium (Ti) implants and subsequent bone formation. Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.
edges.source: Obesity-Induced Modulation of Immune Response:obesity
edges.target: Obesity-Induced Modulation of Immune Response:high-fat diet

Biomaterial surface properties such as roughness and hydrophilicity can influence the behavior of immune cells in the peri-implant microenvironment. This study aimed to determine how obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) affects the inflammatory response to modified titanium (Ti) implants and subsequent bone formation. Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.
edges.source: Obesity-Induced Modulation of Immune Response:high-fat diet
edges.target: Obesity-Induced Modulation of Immune Response:bone formation

Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.

Obesity has been shown to impact the function and activity of neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells, contributing to higher circulating levels of proinflammatory cytokines. Biomaterial surface properties such as roughness and hydrophilicity can influence the behavior of immune cells in the peri-implant microenvironment. This study aimed to determine how obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) affects the inflammatory response to modified titanium (Ti) implants and subsequent bone formation. Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.
edges.source: Obesity-Induced Modulation of Immune Response:bone formation
edges.target: Obesity-Induced Modulation of Immune Response:neutrophils

edges.evidence_phrase: Scanning electron microscopy was used to qualitatively assess surface roughness (SEM, Zeiss Auriga, Carl Zeiss, Germany), and confocal microscopy was used for quantitative assessment (LSM 980 Laser Scanning Microscope, Germany) with a 20x objective and a total measurement area of 790 um x 790 um (n=3). A moving average Gaussian filter with a 30 um cut-off wavelength was used to calculate the arithmetic mean height of the scale-limited surface (Sa). Three samples were used for each surface type to calculate the mean surface roughness at six different areas. A Ram-Hart goniometer was used to measure the sessile drop contact angle to determine surface hydrophilicity (Model 100-25a, Rame-Hart Instrument Co., Succasunna, NJ). 5 uL drops of deionized water were used to measure surface hydrophilicity at six locations per sample (n=3). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a PhI5000 VersaProbe spectrometer (ULVAC-PHI, Inc.) to determine the oxide layer composition. A focused scanning monochromatic Al-Ka source (1,486.6 eV) with a spot size of 200 um was used to acquire spectra, utilizing a base pressure of 1x10 -7  Pa. FAT analyzer mode was used to run the instrument. Detail spectra of 187.85 eV and 46.95 eV were used for the pass energy for survey scans. CasaXPS was utilized for data analysis. Shirley background subtraction was used to integrate the signals. Measurements were taken on each sample in three separate areas.
edges.source: Experimental Methods for Surface and Cellular Analysis:scanning electron microscopy (SEM)
edges.target: Experimental Methods for Surface and Cellular Analysis:confocal microscopy

edges.evidence_phrase: To understand the effect of obesity on the polarization of macrophages in vitro, we cultured bone marrow-derived macrophages from obese or lean mice on rough and roughhydrophilic Ti disks. The proportion of lean-derived pro-inflammatory macrophages was lower in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti surfaces than rough Ti (Figure 10). In contrast, anti-inflammatory macrophages were higher in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti. Obesederived pro-inflammatory macrophages were significantly elevated in response to both surfaces, with the highest populations occurring in response to rough Ti. Obese-derived anti-inflammatory macrophages were lower on all substrates when compared to lean-derived macrophages.
edges.source: Experimental Methods for Surface and Cellular Analysis:in vitro culture of bone marrow-derived macrophages on Ti disks
edges.target: Biomaterial Surface Properties and Macrophage Polarization:rough Ti surface

Obesederived pro-inflammatory macrophages were significantly elevated in response to both surfaces, with the highest populations occurring in response to rough Ti. Obese-derived anti-inflammatory macrophages were lower on all substrates when compared to lean-derived macrophages.
edges.source: Experimental Methods for Surface and Cellular Analysis:in vitro culture of bone marrow-derived macrophages on Ti disks
edges.target: Biomaterial Surface Properties and Macrophage Polarization:anti-inflammatory macrophages

edges.evidence_phrase: Obesity is a metabolic disease resulting from excess body fat accumulation associated with chronic systemic inflammation. Obesity has been shown to impact the function and activity of neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells, contributing to higher circulating levels of proinflammatory cytokines. Biomaterial surface properties such as roughness and hydrophilicity can influence the behavior of immune cells in the peri-implant microenvironment. This study aimed to determine how obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) affects the inflammatory response to modified titanium (Ti) implants and subsequent bone formation. Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.
edges.source: Experimental Methods for Surface and Cellular Analysis:in vivo mouse model of high-fat diet-induced obesity
edges.target: Obesity-Induced Modulation of Immune Response:obesity

edges.evidence_phrase: On day 3 post-implantation, lean mice receiving rough Ti implants showed higher populations of neutrophils, total macrophages, and pro-inflammatory macrophages than rough-hydrophilic Ti (Figure 2). Lean mice receiving rough-hydrophilic Ti implants showed higher populations of anti-inflammatory macrophages when compared to rough Ti. Obese mice demonstrate an altered innate immune response to rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti. On postoperative day 3, total macrophages and pro-inflammatory macrophages were significantly higher in obese mice than in lean mice, while anti-inflammatory macrophages in obese mice were lower.
edges.source: Macrophage Polarization Responses to Implant Surface and Metabolic State:Pro-inflammatory macrophages higher on rough Ti vs rough-hydrophilic Ti in lean mice (day 3)
edges.target: Macrophage Polarization Responses to Implant Surface and Metabolic State:Anti-inflammatory macrophages higher on rough-hydrophilic Ti vs rough Ti in lean mice (day 3)

The proportion of lean-derived pro-inflammatory macrophages was lower in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti surfaces than rough Ti (Figure 10). In contrast, anti-inflammatory macrophages were higher in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti. Obesederived pro-inflammatory macrophages were significantly elevated in response to both surfaces, with the highest populations occurring in response to rough Ti. Obese-derived anti-inflammatory macrophages were lower on all substrates when compared to lean-derived macrophages.
edges.source: Macrophage Polarization Responses to Implant Surface and Metabolic State:Obese-derived pro-inflammatory macrophages elevated on both surfaces, highest on rough Ti in vitro
edges.target: Macrophage Polarization Responses to Implant Surface and Metabolic State:Lean-derived pro-inflammatory macrophages lower on rough-hydrophilic Ti vs rough Ti in vitro

edges.evidence_phrase: Our results show that the inflammatory response to Ti implants is significantly skewed in HFD-induced obesity, with a higher presence of pro-inflammatory factors and immune cells contributing to a chronic inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment. Additionally, macrophage and T cell polarization is shifted towards pro-inflammatory phenotypes, further influencing the inflammatory microenvironment and reducing MSC recruitment and bone formation. Furthermore, our adoptive transfer experiments demonstrated a localized effect of cells isolated from obese mice in the peri-implant microenvironment, suggesting an alteration in the immune cell phenotype and function after the development of obesity via HFD. These findings highlight the impact of obesity on inflammation and healing, dictating the degree of bone formation around the biomaterial implant and, ultimately, the rate of success of implants.
edges.source: Macrophage Polarization Responses to Implant Surface and Metabolic State:Obesity shifts macrophage polarization towards pro-inflammatory phenotypes
edges.target: Inflammatory Cytokine Production and Systemic Inflammation:Obesity creates a chronic inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment

edges.evidence_phrase: Obesity is a metabolic disease resulting from excess body fat accumulation associated with chronic systemic inflammation. Obesity has been shown to impact the function and activity of neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells, contributing to higher circulating levels of proinflammatory cytokines. Biomaterial surface properties such as roughness and hydrophilicity can influence the behavior of immune cells in the peri-implant microenvironment. This study aimed to determine how obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) affects the inflammatory response to modified titanium (Ti) implants and subsequent bone formation. Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.
edges.source: Inflammatory Cytokine Production and Systemic Inflammation:Obesity increases circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines
edges.target: Inflammatory Cytokine Production and Systemic Inflammation:Obesity increases peri-implant neutrophil populations

edges.evidence_phrase: At 7 days post-implantation, lean mice receiving rough Ti implants demonstrated higher populations of neutrophils, total macrophages, and pro-inflammatory macrophages than mice implanted with rough-hydrophilic Ti (Figure 2). Flow cytometry scatter plots for neutrophils, total macrophages, and pro- and anti-inflammatory macrophages at day 7 post-implantation can be found in Supplementary Figure 4.

edges.evidence_phrase: To understand if obesity affects bone formation, we analyzed bone formation at 21 days post-implantation (Figure 8). In lean mice, bone formation was higher on rough-hydrophilic Ti implants than on rough Ti. Obese mice produced significantly less bone around Ti implants than lean mice, and the effect of implant hydrophilicity was abrogated (Figure 8). Additionally, we analyzed tissue mineral density and found that tissue mineral density was higher in lean mice receiving rough-hydrophilic Ti implants when compared to rough Ti (Supplemental Figure 1A). Obese mice demonstrated significantly lower quantities of tissue mineral density when compared to lean mice.
edges.source: Peri-implant Bone Formation and Osseointegration:Bone formation higher on rough-hydrophilic Ti vs rough Ti in lean mice
edges.target: Peri-implant Bone Formation and Osseointegration:Obese mice produce significantly less bone around Ti implants than lean mice

edges.evidence_phrase: Obesity is a metabolic disease resulting from excess body fat accumulation associated with chronic systemic inflammation. Obesity has been shown to impact the function and activity of neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells, contributing to higher circulating levels of proinflammatory cytokines. Biomaterial surface properties such as roughness and hydrophilicity can influence the behavior of immune cells in the peri-implant microenvironment. This study aimed to determine how obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) affects the inflammatory response to modified titanium (Ti) implants and subsequent bone formation. Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.
edges.source: Peri-implant Bone Formation and Osseointegration:Bone formation around Ti implants reduced in obese mice compared to controls
edges.target: Inflammatory Cytokine Production and Systemic Inflammation:Obesity increases circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines

edges.evidence_phrase: Biomaterial surface modifications like roughness and wettability can dictate the recruitment and behavior of immune cells at the implant-tissue interface. Previously, we and others have shown that innate immune cells, including neutrophils and macrophages, activate and polarize in response to differences in Ti physicochemical cues in vitro and in vivo [22,25,32,34,35]. Additionally, we have shown that T cell polarization in vitro and in vivo also responds to changes in biomaterial surface roughness and wettability through interactions with macrophages [21,25,27]. This study demonstrates that environmental factors such as obesity significantly impact the inflammatory response and polarization of immune cells within the peri-implant microenvironment. Our data shows that neutrophils, macrophages, T cells, and MSC recruitment are significantly affected by obesity. Here, we utilized rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants to demonstrate that obesity attenuates the anti-inflammatory and pro-osseointegrative response associated with increasing surface wettability on modified Ti implants.
edges.source: Interaction of Obesity with Surface Bioactivity:Obesity attenuates the anti-inflammatory and pro-osseointegrative response to surface hydrophilicity
edges.target: Interaction of Obesity with Surface Bioactivity:Obesity overrides surface hydrophilicity-driven immunomodulation

edges.evidence_phrase: To understand the effect of obesity on the polarization of macrophages in vitro, we cultured bone marrow-derived macrophages from obese or lean mice on rough and roughhydrophilic Ti disks. The proportion of lean-derived pro-inflammatory macrophages was lower in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti surfaces than rough Ti (Figure 10). In contrast, anti-inflammatory macrophages were higher in response to rough-hydrophilic Ti. Obesederived pro-inflammatory macrophages were significantly elevated in response to both surfaces, with the highest populations occurring in response to rough Ti. Obese-derived anti-inflammatory macrophages were lower on all substrates when compared to lean-derived macrophages.
edges.source: Macrophage Polarization on Titanium Surfaces:rough-hydrophilic titanium surfaces
edges.target: Macrophage Polarization on Titanium Surfaces:rough titanium surfaces

edges.evidence_phrase: Variance in the polarization of CD4 +  T cells provides more context to obesity-induced alterations of the inflammatory response. Th1 and Th17 cells have shown significant elevations in models of diet-induced obesity, along with increased production of their signature pro-inflammatory cytokines, IFNgamma , and IL-17 [10,14,46]. We have demonstrated similar results in our study, showing significantly higher polarization of Th1 and Th17 cells and significantly higher production of the IL-17 isoform IL-17A in obese mice. Our findings show enhanced Th1/Th17 polarization at postoperative day 3, which remains elevated at day 7, demonstrating a prolonged pro-inflammatory T cell response in obese mice. Additionally, we showed that leptin, a hormone derived from adipose tissue, was significantly upregulated in obese mice, which has demonstrated a pro-inflammatory role in the immune system in other studies [51,52] Leptin receptors are highly expressed on T cells, specifically Tregs, and leptin promotes T cell proliferation and increases pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion, skewing T cell polarization towards Th1/Th17 phenotypes and reducing polarization to Th2/ Treg phenotypes [14,16,46]. Tregs are essential for the suppression of the inflammatory response, as prior work suggests that Tregs are more significantly impacted by diet-induced obesity in comparison to other CD4 +  T cell subsets, producing higher levels of reactive oxygen species [16,46,53-55]. Our study demonstrated higher polarization of Tregs in obese mice prior to implantation, with indiscernible changes to the proportion of Tregs through the inflammatory time course, suggesting that the exacerbated inflammatory response associated with obesity may result from inadequate immunomodulation by Tregs.
edges.source: Obesity-Induced Adipokine Disruption:leptin
edges.target: Obesity-Induced Adipokine Disruption:leptin receptor

edges.evidence_phrase: Obesity is a metabolic disease resulting from excess body fat accumulation associated with chronic systemic inflammation. Obesity has been shown to impact the function and activity of neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells, contributing to higher circulating levels of proinflammatory cytokines. Biomaterial surface properties such as roughness and hydrophilicity can influence the behavior of immune cells in the peri-implant microenvironment. This study aimed to determine how obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) affects the inflammatory response to modified titanium (Ti) implants and subsequent bone formation. Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.
edges.source: Obesity-Induced Adipokine Disruption:adipokines
edges.target: Osseointegration Impairment and Immune Dysfunction:bone formation around Ti implants

edges.target: Osseointegration Impairment and Immune Dysfunction:mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs)

edges.evidence_phrase: Obesity is a metabolic disease resulting from excess body fat accumulation associated with chronic systemic inflammation. Obesity has been shown to impact the function and activity of neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells, contributing to higher circulating levels of proinflammatory cytokines. Biomaterial surface properties such as roughness and hydrophilicity can influence the behavior of immune cells in the peri-implant microenvironment. This study aimed to determine how obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) affects the inflammatory response to modified titanium (Ti) implants and subsequent bone formation. Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.

edges.target: Osseointegration Impairment and Immune Dysfunction:adoptive transfer of obese bone marrow cells

edges.evidence_phrase: Variance in the polarization of CD4 +  T cells provides more context to obesity-induced alterations of the inflammatory response. Th1 and Th17 cells have shown significant elevations in models of diet-induced obesity, along with increased production of their signature pro-inflammatory cytokines, IFNgamma , and IL-17 [10,14,46]. We have demonstrated similar results in our study, showing significantly higher polarization of Th1 and Th17 cells and significantly higher production of the IL-17 isoform IL-17A in obese mice. Our findings show enhanced Th1/Th17 polarization at postoperative day 3, which remains elevated at day 7, demonstrating a prolonged pro-inflammatory T cell response in obese mice. Additionally, we showed that leptin, a hormone derived from adipose tissue, was significantly upregulated in obese mice, which has demonstrated a pro-inflammatory role in the immune system in other studies [51,52] Leptin receptors are highly expressed on T cells, specifically Tregs, and leptin promotes T cell proliferation and increases pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion, skewing T cell polarization towards Th1/Th17 phenotypes and reducing polarization to Th2/ Treg phenotypes [14,16,46]. Tregs are essential for the suppression of the inflammatory response, as prior work suggests that Tregs are more significantly impacted by diet-induced obesity in comparison to other CD4 +  T cell subsets, producing higher levels of reactive oxygen species [16,46,53-55]. Our study demonstrated higher polarization of Tregs in obese mice prior to implantation, with indiscernible changes to the proportion of Tregs through the inflammatory time course, suggesting that the exacerbated inflammatory response associated with obesity may result from inadequate immunomodulation by Tregs.
edges.source: Osseointegration Impairment and Immune Dysfunction:Th1 cells
edges.target: Obesity-Induced Adipokine Disruption:leptin

Our study demonstrated higher polarization of Tregs in obese mice prior to implantation, with indiscernible changes to the proportion of Tregs through the inflammatory time course, suggesting that the exacerbated inflammatory response associated with obesity may result from inadequate immunomodulation by Tregs.
edges.source: Osseointegration Impairment and Immune Dysfunction:Th1 cells
edges.target: Obesity-Induced Adipokine Disruption:leptin receptor

We have demonstrated similar results in our study, showing significantly higher polarization of Th1 and Th17 cells and significantly higher production of the IL-17 isoform IL-17A in obese mice. Our findings show enhanced Th1/Th17 polarization at postoperative day 3, which remains elevated at day 7, demonstrating a prolonged pro-inflammatory T cell response in obese mice. Additionally, we showed that leptin, a hormone derived from adipose tissue, was significantly upregulated in obese mice, which has demonstrated a pro-inflammatory role in the immune system in other studies [51,52] Leptin receptors are highly expressed on T cells, specifically Tregs, and leptin promotes T cell proliferation and increases pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion, skewing T cell polarization towards Th1/Th17 phenotypes and reducing polarization to Th2/ Treg phenotypes [14,16,46]. Tregs are essential for the suppression of the inflammatory response, as prior work suggests that Tregs are more significantly impacted by diet-induced obesity in comparison to other CD4 +  T cell subsets, producing higher levels of reactive oxygen species [16,46,53-55]. Our study demonstrated higher polarization of Tregs in obese mice prior to implantation, with indiscernible changes to the proportion of Tregs through the inflammatory time course, suggesting that the exacerbated inflammatory response associated with obesity may result from inadequate immunomodulation by Tregs.
edges.source: Osseointegration Impairment and Immune Dysfunction:Treg cells
edges.target: Osseointegration Impairment and Immune Dysfunction:Th17 cells

edges.evidence_phrase: Obesity is a metabolic disease resulting from excess body fat accumulation associated with chronic systemic inflammation. Obesity has been shown to impact the function and activity of neutrophils, macrophages, and T cells, contributing to higher circulating levels of proinflammatory cytokines. Biomaterial surface properties such as roughness and hydrophilicity can influence the behavior of immune cells in the peri-implant microenvironment. This study aimed to determine how obesity induced by a high-fat diet (HFD) affects the inflammatory response to modified titanium (Ti) implants and subsequent bone formation. Obese mice had significantly more neutrophils, pro-inflammatory macrophages, and T cells and fewer anti-inflammatory macrophages and mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in the peri-implant tissue than lean mice. Obesity also increased circulating adipokines and pro-inflammatory cytokines when compared to lean animals. Bone formation around Ti implants was reduced in obese mice compared to controls. Adoptive transfer of bone marrow cells isolated from obese mice into wild-type mice demonstrated the localized impact of obesity on immune cell function and phenotype, promoting a pro-inflammatory peri-implant microenvironment and attenuating bone formation post-implantation. These results show that obesity significantly affects the inflammatory response to modified Ti implants, prolonging the pro-inflammatory response to the implanted biomaterial and compromising bone formation.
edges.source: Osseointegration Impairment and Immune Dysfunction:adoptive transfer of obese bone marrow cells
edges.target: Obesity-Induced Adipokine Disruption:adipokines

edges.evidence_phrase: Variance in the polarization of CD4 +  T cells provides more context to obesity-induced alterations of the inflammatory response. Th1 and Th17 cells have shown significant elevations in models of diet-induced obesity, along with increased production of their signature pro-inflammatory cytokines, IFNgamma , and IL-17 [10,14,46]. We have demonstrated similar results in our study, showing significantly higher polarization of Th1 and Th17 cells and significantly higher production of the IL-17 isoform IL-17A in obese mice. Our findings show enhanced Th1/Th17 polarization at postoperative day 3, which remains elevated at day 7, demonstrating a prolonged pro-inflammatory T cell response in obese mice. Additionally, we showed that leptin, a hormone derived from adipose tissue, was significantly upregulated in obese mice, which has demonstrated a pro-inflammatory role in the immune system in other studies [51,52] Leptin receptors are highly expressed on T cells, specifically Tregs, and leptin promotes T cell proliferation and increases pro-inflammatory cytokine secretion, skewing T cell polarization towards Th1/Th17 phenotypes and reducing polarization to Th2/ Treg phenotypes [14,16,46]. Tregs are essential for the suppression of the inflammatory response, as prior work suggests that Tregs are more significantly impacted by diet-induced obesity in comparison to other CD4 +  T cell subsets, producing higher levels of reactive oxygen species [16,46,53-55]. Our study demonstrated higher polarization of Tregs in obese mice prior to implantation, with indiscernible changes to the proportion of Tregs through the inflammatory time course, suggesting that the exacerbated inflammatory response associated with obesity may result from inadequate immunomodulation by Tregs.
edges.source: Signaling Pathway Hypotheses:leptin signaling
edges.target: Obesity-Induced Adipokine Disruption:leptin

edges.evidence_phrase: Macrophages play a central role in orchestrating the inflammatory response to implanted biomaterials and are sensitive to changes in the chemical and physical characteristics of the implant. Macrophages respond to biological, chemical, and physical cues by polarizing into pro-inflammatory (M1) or anti-inflammatory (M2) states. We previously showed that roughhydrophilic titanium (Ti) implants skew macrophage polarization towards an anti-inflammatory phenotype and increase mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) recruitment and bone formation around the implant. In the present study, we aimed to investigate whether the adoptive transfer of macrophages in different polarization states would alter the inflammatory microenvironment and improve biomaterial integration in macrophage-competent and macrophage-ablated mice. We found that ablating macrophages increased the presence of neutrophils, reduced T cells and MSCs, and compromised the healing and biomaterial integration process. These effects could not be rescued with adoptive transfer of nave or polarized macrophages. Adoptive transfer of M1 macrophages into macrophage-competent mice increased inflammatory cells and inflammatory microenvironment, resulting in decreased bone-to-implant contact. Adoptive transfer of M2 macrophages into macrophage-competent mice reduced the pro-inflammatory environment in the peri-implant tissue and increased bone-to-implant contact. Taken together, our results show the importance of macrophages in controlling and modulating the inflammatory process in response to implanted biomaterials and suggest they can be used to improve outcomes following biomaterial implantation.
edges.source: Surface Modification Characteristics:rough-hydrophilic Ti implants
edges.target: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:MSCs

edges.target: Tissue Healing and Structural Outcomes:bone formation

edges.evidence_phrase: Increased surface roughness and topography are evident in SEM images on rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti Implants when compared to smooth implants (Figure 1). Assessment of surface average roughness (Sa, Figure 1A) indicated higher roughness for rough Ti implants (Sa= 3.33 um) and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants (Sa= 3.31 um) compared to smooth Ti implants (0.6 um). No qualitative or quantitative differences were observed in roughness between rough and rough-hydrophilic implants. Rough-hydrophilic implants had the highest wettability with a contact angle ( theta ) of 0deg (Figure 1B). Rough Ti implants were the most hydrophobic ( theta = 86deg), and smooth implants were relatively hydrophobic ( theta = 62deg). Rough-hydrophilic implants had the lowest carbon content and the highest levels of oxygen and titanium of the implants tested (Figure 1C).
edges.source: Surface Modification Characteristics:rough Ti implants
edges.target: Surface Modification Characteristics:smooth Ti implants

edges.evidence_phrase: We, and others, have shown that macrophages are sensitive to changes in physicochemical properties on the biomaterial surface in vivo and that these physicochemical properties can skew macrophage phenotypes [24]. These physicochemical properties not only control macrophage phenotype but also dictate the speed of the healing response and, in some cases, the fate of the implanted material [22,27,28]. In the specific case of Ti implants, we have shown that rough-hydrophilic Ti implants skew macrophage polarization toward an anti-inflammatory or M2-like phenotype and are capable of polarizing CD4 +  T cells into Th2 or regulatory T cells (Treg), cells critical for immunomodulation of the inflammatory process, resulting in faster healing and osseointegration [26,29,30]. We have shown that smooth-hydrophobic Ti implants skew macrophages into a pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype and polarize CD4+ T cells into Th1 or Th17 cells, which extends the inflammatory process and the healing time [26,30].
edges.source: Surface Modification Characteristics:smooth-hydrophobic Ti implants
edges.target: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:M1 macrophages

edges.evidence_phrase: Increased surface roughness and topography are evident in SEM images on rough and rough-hydrophilic Ti Implants when compared to smooth implants (Figure 1). Assessment of surface average roughness (Sa, Figure 1A) indicated higher roughness for rough Ti implants (Sa= 3.33 um) and rough-hydrophilic Ti implants (Sa= 3.31 um) compared to smooth Ti implants (0.6 um). No qualitative or quantitative differences were observed in roughness between rough and rough-hydrophilic implants. Rough-hydrophilic implants had the highest wettability with a contact angle ( theta ) of 0deg (Figure 1B). Rough Ti implants were the most hydrophobic ( theta = 86deg), and smooth implants were relatively hydrophobic ( theta = 62deg). Rough-hydrophilic implants had the lowest carbon content and the highest levels of oxygen and titanium of the implants tested (Figure 1C).
edges.source: Surface Modification Characteristics:contact angle
edges.target: Surface Modification Characteristics:carbon content

edges.evidence_phrase: We, and others, have shown that macrophages are sensitive to changes in physicochemical properties on the biomaterial surface in vivo and that these physicochemical properties can skew macrophage phenotypes [24]. These physicochemical properties not only control macrophage phenotype but also dictate the speed of the healing response and, in some cases, the fate of the implanted material [22,27,28]. In the specific case of Ti implants, we have shown that rough-hydrophilic Ti implants skew macrophage polarization toward an anti-inflammatory or M2-like phenotype and are capable of polarizing CD4 +  T cells into Th2 or regulatory T cells (Treg), cells critical for immunomodulation of the inflammatory process, resulting in faster healing and osseointegration [26,29,30]. We have shown that smooth-hydrophobic Ti implants skew macrophages into a pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype and polarize CD4+ T cells into Th1 or Th17 cells, which extends the inflammatory process and the healing time [26,30].
edges.source: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:M1 macrophages
edges.target: Surface Modification Characteristics:smooth-hydrophobic Ti implants

In the specific case of Ti implants, we have shown that rough-hydrophilic Ti implants skew macrophage polarization toward an anti-inflammatory or M2-like phenotype and are capable of polarizing CD4 +  T cells into Th2 or regulatory T cells (Treg), cells critical for immunomodulation of the inflammatory process, resulting in faster healing and osseointegration [26,29,30]. edges.target: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:M2 macrophages

In the specific case of Ti implants, we have shown that rough-hydrophilic Ti implants skew macrophage polarization toward an anti-inflammatory or M2-like phenotype and are capable of polarizing CD4 +  T cells into Th2 or regulatory T cells (Treg), cells critical for immunomodulation of the inflammatory process, resulting in faster healing and osseointegration [26,29,30]. We have shown that smooth-hydrophobic Ti implants skew macrophages into a pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype and polarize CD4+ T cells into Th1 or Th17 cells, which extends the inflammatory process and the healing time [26,30].
edges.source: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:M1 macrophages
edges.target: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:Th17 cells

edges.evidence_phrase: In C57BL/6J mice, neutrophils were slightly lower on rough-hydrophilic Ti than smooth or rough implants (Figure 2). MaFIA mice had more peri-implant neutrophils than C57BL/6J mice on all Ti implants (Figure 2). C57BL/6J mice showed decreased T cells on roughhydrophilic implants compared to smooth and rough implants. We observed a significant decrease in T cells in MaFIA mice without differences between the Ti implants tested. From the T cells, we also observed in MaFIA mice an increase in CD4 +  T cells and a decrease in CD8 +  T cells in all implants tested. In C57BL/6J mice, we observed increased CD4 + and CD8 +  T cells on rough-hydrophilic Ti compared to smooth and rough Ti modifications. Ablation of macrophages dramatically altered the effect of Ti surface modifications on neutrophils and T cells, as observed in C57BL/6J mice, where rough-hydrophilic Ti reduced neutrophil presence and increased CD4 +  and CD8 +  T cells (Figure 2). MSC recruitment was minimal at this time point, and macrophage ablation did not affect the proportion of MSCs at this time point.
edges.source: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:CD4+ T cells
edges.target: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:CD8+ T cells

edges.evidence_phrase: Macrophages play a central role in orchestrating the inflammatory response to implanted biomaterials and are sensitive to changes in the chemical and physical characteristics of the implant. Macrophages respond to biological, chemical, and physical cues by polarizing into pro-inflammatory (M1) or anti-inflammatory (M2) states. We previously showed that roughhydrophilic titanium (Ti) implants skew macrophage polarization towards an anti-inflammatory phenotype and increase mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) recruitment and bone formation around the implant. In the present study, we aimed to investigate whether the adoptive transfer of macrophages in different polarization states would alter the inflammatory microenvironment and improve biomaterial integration in macrophage-competent and macrophage-ablated mice. We found that ablating macrophages increased the presence of neutrophils, reduced T cells and MSCs, and compromised the healing and biomaterial integration process. These effects could not be rescued with adoptive transfer of nave or polarized macrophages. Adoptive transfer of M1 macrophages into macrophage-competent mice increased inflammatory cells and inflammatory microenvironment, resulting in decreased bone-to-implant contact. Adoptive transfer of M2 macrophages into macrophage-competent mice reduced the pro-inflammatory environment in the peri-implant tissue and increased bone-to-implant contact. Taken together, our results show the importance of macrophages in controlling and modulating the inflammatory process in response to implanted biomaterials and suggest they can be used to improve outcomes following biomaterial implantation.
edges.source: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:MSCs
edges.target: Surface Modification Characteristics:rough-hydrophilic Ti implants

edges.evidence_phrase: Macrophages play a significant role in the inflammatory response following biomaterial implantation, eliminating cellular and tissue debris from the surgical procedure, modulating the inflammatory milieu by regulating cytokine and chemokine release, and recruiting other immune cells. The physicochemical properties of the surface of the implanted biomaterial have been shown to alter macrophage activation and phenotype [22-25]. Our group has demonstrated that macrophages cultured on rough-hydrophilic titanium (Ti) substrates preferentially activate into an M2-like phenotype with higher arginase expression and IL-10 secretion compared to macrophages cultured on hydrophobic Ti substrates, which preferentially activate macrophages into a M1-like phenotype with high secretion of IL-1 beta , IL-6, and TNFalpha , and high expression of nitric oxide synthase (Nos2) [2426]. Importantly, these effects occur in vitro, where damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), cytokines, and lipid inflammatory mediators from surrounding tissues and cells are absent [22-24].
edges.source: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:IL-10
edges.target: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:arginase

edges.target: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:IL-1beta

edges.evidence_phrase: Macrophages play a significant role in the inflammatory response following biomaterial implantation, eliminating cellular and tissue debris from the surgical procedure, modulating the inflammatory milieu by regulating cytokine and chemokine release, and recruiting other immune cells. The physicochemical properties of the surface of the implanted biomaterial have been shown to alter macrophage activation and phenotype [22-25]. Our group has demonstrated that macrophages cultured on rough-hydrophilic titanium (Ti) substrates preferentially activate into an M2-like phenotype with higher arginase expression and IL-10 secretion compared to macrophages cultured on hydrophobic Ti substrates, which preferentially activate macrophages into a M1-like phenotype with high secretion of IL-1 beta , IL-6, and TNFalpha , and high expression of nitric oxide synthase (Nos2) [2426]. Importantly, these effects occur in vitro, where damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), cytokines, and lipid inflammatory mediators from surrounding tissues and cells are absent [22-24].
edges.source: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:IL-10
edges.target: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:TNF-alpha

edges.evidence_phrase: We also were interested in how obesity affects the polarization of CD4 +  T cell subsets that contribute to inflammation in the peri-implant microenvironment. Pre-implantation, obese mice showed higher polarization of Th1 and CD4 +  Tregs than lean mice (Supplemental Figure 3). Populations of Th2 and Th17 were significantly lower in obese mice than in lean mice.

edges.target: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:Treg cells in obesity

edges.evidence_phrase: Macrophages play a central role in orchestrating the inflammatory response to implanted biomaterials and are sensitive to changes in the chemical and physical characteristics of the implant. Macrophages respond to biological, chemical, and physical cues by polarizing into pro-inflammatory (M1) or anti-inflammatory (M2) states. We previously showed that roughhydrophilic titanium (Ti) implants skew macrophage polarization towards an anti-inflammatory phenotype and increase mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) recruitment and bone formation around the implant. In the present study, we aimed to investigate whether the adoptive transfer of macrophages in different polarization states would alter the inflammatory microenvironment and improve biomaterial integration in macrophage-competent and macrophage-ablated mice. We found that ablating macrophages increased the presence of neutrophils, reduced T cells and MSCs, and compromised the healing and biomaterial integration process. These effects could not be rescued with adoptive transfer of nave or polarized macrophages. Adoptive transfer of M1 macrophages into macrophage-competent mice increased inflammatory cells and inflammatory microenvironment, resulting in decreased bone-to-implant contact. Adoptive transfer of M2 macrophages into macrophage-competent mice reduced the pro-inflammatory environment in the peri-implant tissue and increased bone-to-implant contact. Taken together, our results show the importance of macrophages in controlling and modulating the inflammatory process in response to implanted biomaterials and suggest they can be used to improve outcomes following biomaterial implantation.
edges.source: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:neutrophils in macrophage ablation
edges.target: Surface Modification Characteristics:rough-hydrophilic Ti implants

edges.evidence_phrase: Like neutrophils and macrophages, T cells have also been investigated in animal models of HFD-induced obesity but have not been explored in the context of bone healing in obese subjects. Both CD4 +  and CD8 +  T cell subsets are essential for resolving inflammation and bone regeneration in the context of implantation, as the ablation of these subsets results in reduced MSC recruitment and proliferation and reduced bone formation around the biomaterial implant [21,27]. CD4 +  and CD8 +  T cell populations are significantly higher in models of HFD-induced obesity, and these subsets have demonstrated different roles in amplifying systemic inflammation [8-10,16,46]. CD8 +  T cell populations are present in higher quantities in mice fed HFD, infiltrating and interacting with adipose tissue before other immune cells [8,47]. While it is unknown why these cells are recruited in the adipose tissue before other cell types, CD8 +  T cells increase the recruitment of pro-inflammatory macrophages and the production of TNFalpha [8,9]. Elevated CD8 +  T cell populations are associated with poor fracture healing and lack of bone formation [48]. In our study, CD8 +  T cells were higher in obese mice prior to implantation, as well as all time points post-implantation. Similarly, CD4 +  T cells were higher in obese mice on day 7 post-implantation but lower at earlier times. Prior studies examining CD4 +  T cells in obesity observe significantly lower production of IL-2, which impairs the recruitment and proliferation of CD4 +  T cells in the early inflammatory process [49,50].
edges.source: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:CD8+ T cells in obesity
edges.target: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:CD4+ T cells in obesity (day 7)

In the present study, we aimed to investigate whether the adoptive transfer of macrophages in different polarization states would alter the inflammatory microenvironment and improve biomaterial integration in macrophage-competent and macrophage-ablated mice. We found that ablating macrophages increased the presence of neutrophils, reduced T cells and MSCs, and compromised the healing and biomaterial integration process. These effects could not be rescued with adoptive transfer of nave or polarized macrophages. Adoptive transfer of M1 macrophages into macrophage-competent mice increased inflammatory cells and inflammatory microenvironment, resulting in decreased bone-to-implant contact. Adoptive transfer of M2 macrophages into macrophage-competent mice reduced the pro-inflammatory environment in the peri-implant tissue and increased bone-to-implant contact. Taken together, our results show the importance of macrophages in controlling and modulating the inflammatory process in response to implanted biomaterials and suggest they can be used to improve outcomes following biomaterial implantation.
edges.source: Tissue Healing and Structural Outcomes:bone-to-implant contact
edges.target: Immune Cell Polarization Responses:neutrophils in macrophage ablation
